# Liveblocks Collaborative Kanban Board (Next.js)

## Background

This Next.js project contains a scaffold for a real-time collaborative Kanban board powered by https://liveblocks.io/. The board has three fixed columns (`To Do`, `In Progress`, `Done`) and stores its data inside a shared Liveblocks room so that any change made by one user is instantly synchronized to every other user in the same room. The scaffold already renders the empty columns and wires up the `RoomProvider`, but it does not yet implement card creation, editing, deletion, or drag-and-drop movement between columns.

Your job is to finish the Kanban application using **Liveblocks Storage** (`LiveList`, `LiveObject`, `LiveMap`) and the `useMutation` hook. The Liveblocks **cloud service is used directly** — there is no mocking. The provided keys belong to a real Liveblocks project.

## Requirements

Implement the following features inside the scaffold at `/home/user/myproject`:

1. **Shared storage schema** (do not change the keys/shape):
   * `Storage.columns: LiveList<LiveObject<{ id: string; title: string; cardIds: LiveList<string> }>>` initialized with exactly three columns whose `id` values are `todo`, `in-progress`, `done` and whose `title` values are `To Do`, `In Progress`, `Done` (in that order).
   * `Storage.cards: LiveMap<string, LiveObject<{ id: string; title: string; description: string; assignee: string }>>`.
2. **Add Card**: Each column has an input + button (or equivalent) that adds a new card to that column. The new card's `id` must be appended to that column's `cardIds` and the card itself must be inserted into `Storage.cards`.
3. **Inline edit card title**: Clicking the card title turns it into an editable input. Saving (Enter or blur) writes the new title back to the corresponding `LiveObject` in `Storage.cards`.
4. **Delete card**: Each card has a delete control that removes the card's id from its column's `cardIds` and deletes the entry from `Storage.cards`.
5. **Drag-and-drop between columns**: Cards can be moved between any two columns using drag-and-drop (HTML5 DnD or `react-dnd`, your choice). The move **MUST** happen inside a single `useMutation` call that (a) removes the card id from the source column's `cardIds` and (b) pushes/inserts it into the target column's `cardIds`, so the operation is batched as a single Liveblocks transaction.
6. **Real-time sync via Liveblocks cloud**: All changes must be performed through Liveblocks Storage (no local-only state). Two browser tabs joined to the same room must see each other's changes in real time.
7. **Auth endpoint**: Implement (or keep) a server route at `POST /api/liveblocks-auth` that calls `Liveblocks.prepareSession` (or `identifyUser`) using the `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` environment variable and grants full access to rooms whose id starts with `kanban-`.
8. **Room id**: The board page must join the Liveblocks room `kanban-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}`. The harness only injects `ZEALT_RUN_ID` (server-side) into the container; the browser bundle cannot read it directly. You **MUST** mirror it into the client by re-exporting it as `NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID` (e.g. set `env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID = process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID` inside `next.config.js`, or write it into `.env`/`.env.local` during build, so that `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID === process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID` at runtime). Fall back to the literal string `kanban-local` only when neither variable is set. The room id **must include the run-id suffix** so concurrent evaluations don't collide.

## Implementation Hints

* Use `@liveblocks/client`, `@liveblocks/react`, and `@liveblocks/node` (already installed).
* Define your Storage types via `liveblocks.config.ts` and read storage with selector hooks (`useStorage`) and write with `useMutation`.
* For the drag mutation, look at `LiveList.delete(index)` / `LiveList.push(value)` / `LiveList.insert(value, index)` from `@liveblocks/client`. Performing both operations inside the same `useMutation` callback automatically batches them into one Liveblocks update.
* The auth endpoint should use `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` only for the public client; the secret key must stay server-side.
* `Storage` is initialized via the `RoomProvider`'s `initialStorage` prop — make sure the three columns are created exactly once at first load.
* Do **NOT** mock Liveblocks. Use the real cloud.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Start command: `npm run start` (the scaffold provides `npm run build` followed by `npm run start`; you may also run `npm run dev` during development)
* Port: 3000
* Routes:
  * `GET /` — Renders the Kanban board UI. The page must visibly contain three columns titled `To Do`, `In Progress`, and `Done`.
  * `POST /api/liveblocks-auth` — Liveblocks authentication endpoint. Returns a Liveblocks auth token JSON payload for rooms prefixed with `kanban-`.
* Storage shape (as observed via Liveblocks selectors):
  * `root.columns` is an array of length 3 with `id` values `todo`, `in-progress`, `done` and `title` values `To Do`, `In Progress`, `Done` in that order.
  * `root.cards` is a map keyed by card id.
* Behaviour (verified through two real browser contexts hitting the same room):
  * User A can add a new card to a column; user B sees the same card appear in that column without reloading.
  * User A can drag the card from `To Do` to `In Progress`; user B sees the card disappear from `To Do` and appear in `In Progress` within a few seconds.
  * User A can edit a card title inline; user B sees the updated title.
  * User A can delete a card; user B sees the card removed.
* Room id format: the page joins the room whose id is `kanban-${ZEALT_RUN_ID}`. The agent must re-export `ZEALT_RUN_ID` as `NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID` (e.g. via `next.config.js` `env`) so the client bundle can read it. `NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID` must mirror `ZEALT_RUN_ID` exactly.
* The drag-and-drop move between columns **MUST** be implemented as a single `useMutation` callback that batches the source-column removal and the target-column insertion into one Liveblocks transaction (verifiable by inspecting the source: only one `useMutation` for the move, calling both `LiveList.delete` and `LiveList.push`/`insert` on the column lists).
Let me start by understanding the project structure and existing code, then implement all the required features.

todos.content: Explore project structure and understand existing scaffold
Set up NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID in next.config.js
Define Liveblocks storage schema and config
Implement auth endpoint at POST /api/liveblocks-auth
Implement Kanban board with columns, cards, add/edit/delete/drag-drop
Build and test the application

# prefix (e.g. via next.config.js `env` or by writing it into the build env)

2. Update `liveblocks.config.ts` to define Storage types and typed hooks
3. Update the auth endpoint at `/api/liveblocks-auth/route.ts`
4. Implement the main Kanban page with all features (add card, inline edit, delete, drag-and-drop)
5. Create the `.env.local` file with the Liveblocks keys (or use the env vars)

* Prepares a new session to authorize a user to access Liveblocks.
     *
     * IMPORTANT:
     * Always make sure that you trust the user making the request to your
     * backend before calling .prepareSession()!
     *
     * @param userId Tell Liveblocks the user ID of the user to authorize. Must
     * uniquely identify the user account in your system. The uniqueness of this
     * value will determine how many MAUs will be counted/billed.
     *
     * @param options.userInfo Custom metadata to attach to this user. Data you
     * add here will be visible to all other clients in the room, through the
     * `other.info` property.
     *
     */
    prepareSession(userId: string, ...rest: OptionalTupleUnless<CreateSessionOptions<U>, [
        options: CreateSessionOptions<U>
    ]>): Session;
    /**
     * Call this to authenticate the user as an actor you want to allow to use
     * Liveblocks.
     *
     * You should use this method only if you want to manage your permissions
     * through the Liveblocks Permissions API. This method is more complicated to
     * set up, but allows for finer-grained specification of permissions.
     *
     * Calling `.identifyUser()` only lets you securely identify a user (and what
     * groups they belong to). What permissions this user will end up having is
     * determined by whatever permissions you assign the user/group in your
     * Liveblocks account, through the Permissions API:
     * https://liveblocks.io/docs/rooms/permissions
     *
     * IMPORTANT:
     * Always verify that you trust the user making the request before calling
     * .identifyUser()!
     *
     * @param identity Tell Liveblocks the user ID of the user to authenticate.
     * Must uniquely identify the user account in your system. The uniqueness of
     * this value will determine how many MAUs will be counted/billed.
     *
     * If you also want to assign which groups this user belongs to, use the
     * object form and specify the `groupIds` property. Those `groupIds` should
     * match the groupIds you assigned permissions to via the Liveblocks
     * Permissions API, see
     * https://liveblocks.io/docs/rooms/permissions#permissions-levels-groups-accesses-example
     *
     * @param options.userInfo Custom metadata to attach to this user. Data you
     * add here will be visible to all other clients in the room, through the
     * `other.info` property.
     */
    identifyUser(identity: string | Identity, ...rest: OptionalTupleUnless<IdentifyUserOptions<U>, [
        options: IdentifyUserOptions<U>
    ]>): Promise<AuthResponse>;

     * Returns a list of your rooms. The rooms are returned sorted by creation date, from newest to oldest. You can filter rooms by metadata, users accesses and groups accesses.
     * @param params.limit (optional) A limit on the number of rooms to be returned. The limit can range between 1 and 100, and defaults to 20.
     * @param params.startingAfter (optional) A cursor used for pagination. You get the value from the response of the previous page.
     * @param params.userId (optional) A filter on users accesses.
     * @param params.metadata (optional) A filter on metadata. Multiple metadata keys can be used to filter rooms.
     * @param params.groupIds (optional) A filter on groups accesses. Multiple groups can be used.
     * @param params.query (optional) A query to filter rooms by. It is based on our query language. You can filter by metadata and room ID.

The import path is wrong. The file is at `/home/user/myproject/liveblocks.config.ts` and the page is at `/home/user/myproject/app/page.tsx`. The relative path from `app/page.tsx` to `liveblocks.config.ts` would be `../liveblocks.config`. Let me fix this.

Actually wait, in Next.js with the app router, the import path resolution depends on the project structure. Let me check the tsconfig for path aliases.

The tsconfig has `"paths": { "@/*": ["./*"] }`, so I can use `@/liveblocks.config`.

Or I can just use the relative path `../liveblocks.config`.

part.text:
The `drag` ref from `useDrag` needs to be applied differently. In react-dnd, the `drag` connector function needs to be called with a DOM element or used with `ref` differently. The issue is that `ref={drag}` on a `<div>` doesn't work directly because the type is `ConnectDragSource` which returns a `ReactElement`.

I need to use a `useRef` and combine it with the drag connector. Let me fix this by using a callback ref pattern or by wrapping the drag ref.
The `ref={drag}` pattern doesn't work directly with `<div>` due to type issues. I need to use a callback ref approach:
Now I need to fix the `ref` usage. The `drag` and `drop` connectors from react-dnd are functions that need to be called with DOM elements. I'll use a callbackref pattern:
Now fix the column'sdrop ref:

input.searchContent:
<div
      ref={drop}
      style={{
        border: "1px solid #ddd",
        borderRadius: 8,
        padding: 12,
        minHeight: 240,
        backgroundColor: isOver ? "#f0f7ff" : "#f9f9f9",
        display: "flex",
        flexDirection: "column",
      }}
      data-column-id={columnId}
    >
input.replaceContent:
<div
      ref={(node) => {
        drop(node);
        // @ts-expect-error - react-dnd ref compatibility
        colRef.current = node;
      }}
      style={{
        border: "1px solid #ddd",
        borderRadius: 8,
        padding: 12,
        minHeight: 240,
        backgroundColor: isOver ? "#f0f7ff" : "#f9f9f9",
        display: "flex",
        flexDirection: "column",
      }}
      data-column-id={columnId}
    >

part.text: The `@ts-expect-error` directive is unused because the assignment is actually valid. Let me remove those comments and also remove the unused refs and import.
The `@ts-expect-error` comments are unnecessary. Let me clean up thecode:

I need to fix the `@ts-expect-error` comments and also remove unused imports. Let me rewrite the whole file cleanly. The issue is that `ref` callback functions on HTMLDivElement elements don't need `@ts-expect-error` because the assignment to a ref's `.current` is valid. Let me also remove the unused `cardRef`, `colRef`, `inputRef`, `ConnectDragSource`, `ConnectDropTarget` imports, and fix the room ID logic.

Actually, looking more carefully at the code, the `cardRef` and `colRef` refs are never used for anything other than storing the node reference, so they're unnecessary. Let me just simplify the ref callbacks to only call the drag/drop connectors.

Also, the `inputRef` is never used. And `ConnectDragSource` and `ConnectDropTarget` types are imported but unused.

Let me also fix the room ID logic. The current logic is:
```
process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID || process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID
  ? `kanban-${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID || process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID}`
  : "kanban-local"
```

This is wrong because of operator precedence! `||` has higher precedence than `?`, so this reads as:
```
(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID || process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID)
  ? `kanban-${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID || process.env.ZEALT_RUN_ID}`
  : "kanban-local"
```

Which means: if NEXT_PUBLIC_ZEALT_RUN_ID or ZEALT_RUN_ID is truthy, use the kanban prefix, otherwise use kanban-local. That's actually correct logic, but it's confusing. Let me make it clearer.

The `LiveList.insert` expects the index to be a `number`, but the TypeScript type definition seems to require a `string`. Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me check the LiveList API. Maybe the `insert` method has a different signature.

Actually, looking at the `@liveblocks/client` types that were exported, `LiveList` is exported from `@liveblocks/core`. Let me check the actual type of the `insert` method.

Actually, the issue might be that `LiveList<string>` has an `insert` method that takes `(index: number, value: string)`. But wait, the error says the argument of type `number` is not assignable to parameter of type `string`. So maybe the `insert` method has a different signature.

Let me check the type definitions morecarefully.

The `insert` method signature is `insert(element: TItem, index: number): void;`. So the element comes first, then the index. I had it backwards in my code. Let me fix this - I was calling `targetCardIds.insert(insertAt, cardId)` but it should be `targetCardIds.insert(cardId, insertAt)`.

Wait, let me re-read the error message:
```
./app/page.tsx:279:28
Type error: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'.
```

Line 279 is `targetCardIds.insert(insertAt, cardId);`

Since `insert(element: TItem, index: number)` where TItem is `string`, the first argument should be `cardId` (a string) and the second should be `insertAt` (a number). I had them reversed!

Let me fix this.
The `LiveList.insert` method takes `(element, index)` not `(index, element)`. Let me fix the parameterorder:
